retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: As the most common cause of respiratory distress in premature infants, RDS occurs in about 24,000 infants born in the United States annually. It is also the most common complication of prematurity leading to significant morbidity in late preterm neonates and even mortality in very low birth weight infants. The exact definition of RDS is imprecise, thus requiring a careful analysis of statistical data. The most important risk factors are prematurity and low birth weight. Other risk factors include white race, male gender, late preterm delivery, maternal diabetes, perinatal hypoxia and ischemia, and delivery in the absence of labor. [13] The incidence of RDS increases with decreasing gestational age at birth. In one study of babies born between 2003 and 2007 at various National Institute of Child Health and Human Development (NICHD) Neonatal Research Network centers, 98% of babies born at 24 weeks had RDS, while at 34 weeks, the incidence was 5%, and at 37 weeks was less than 1%. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal respiratory distress syndrome, or RDS, is a common cause of respiratory distress in a newborn, presenting within hours after birth, most often immediately after delivery. RDS primarily affects preterm neonates, and infrequently, term infants. The incidence of RDS is inversely proportional to the gestational age of the infant, with more severe disease in the smaller and more premature neonates. While treatment modalities, including antenatal corticosteroids, surfactants, and advanced respiratory care of the neonate, have improved the outcomes for patients affected by RDS, it continues to be a leading cause of morbidity and mortality in the preterm infant.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome -- Etiology -- Fetal Lung Development
retrieved_docs.content: At 32 weeks, the alveolar stage begins, and alveoli start to form as the respiratory bronchioles develop septations, increasing the surface area for gas exchange. By term age, the lung contains 50 to 150 million alveoli.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Lung -- Clinical Significance -- Infant Respiratory Distress
retrieved_docs.content: Infant respiratory distress is the leading cause of death in premature babies. [37] Type II pneumocytes produce surfactant starting around 20 weeks of gestation, but its full secretion does not occur until nearly 30 weeks of pregnancy. Without ample surfactant, premature infants cannot overcome the collapsing surface tension in the respiratory alveoli. When a patient goes into premature labor, Clinicians administer glucocorticoids to help prevent infant respiratory distress. [38] Glucocorticoids stimulate surfactant production in the fetus and may increase its production enough to help the infant overcome any potential respiratory distress. Testing for fetal lung maturity may be performed on the amniotic fluid by several methods, including the measurement of phospholipids in amniotic fluid (phosphatidylglycerol or lecithin-sphingomyelin ratio) and lamellar body counts.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome -- Etiology -- Fetal Lung Development
retrieved_docs.content: During the saccular stage, from around the 24th week to the 32nd week of gestation, the terminal saccule forms, developing the respiratory bronchioles, which have a wall thickness that allows for gas exchange. It is at this stage that the premature infant is potentially viable in extrauterine life. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Alveolar Tension -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Thankfully, many interventions exist nowadays to help patients with neonatal respiratory distress syndrome. These treatment options aim to decrease the severity of the disease or to prevent its occurrence in the first place. Prevention wise, all pregnant women at risk of preterm delivery should be given antenatal glucocorticoids to help enhance fetal lung maturity and surfactant production. [7] For neonates with the disease, respiratory support with positive end-expiratory pressure helps keep the airways open at end-expiration and prevents alveolar collapse. [8] Finally, exogenous surfactant replacement therapy now exists to help improve pulmonary function in neonates with RDS. It works by replacing surfactant temporarily until the lungs mature enough to produce it on their own. As mentioned previously, a wide variety of other pulmonary diseases may cause or manifest with abnormalities in surfactant production, function, or metabolism. Regrettably, at this time, there is no routine use for exogenous surfactant in these diseases. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Prognosis of infants managed with antenatal steroids, respiratory support, and exogenous surfactant therapy is excellent. Mortality is less than 10%, with some studies showing survival rates of up to 98% with advanced care. Increased survival in developed countries is in stark comparison to babies who received no intervention in low-income countries, where the mortality rate for premature infants with RDS is significantly higher, at times close to 100%. [40] With adequate ventilatory support alone, surfactant production eventually begins, and once surfactant production begins along with the onset of diuresis, RDS improves within 4 or 5 days. Untreated disease leading to severe hypoxemia in the first days of life can result in multiple organ failure and death.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Neurodevelopmental delay is another complication of RDS, especially with infants who received mechanical ventilation long-term. [42] The incidence of cerebral palsy also was increased in infants with RDS, with decreasing incidence as gestational age increased. The length of time on mechanical ventilation correlates with increased rates of both cerebral palsy and neurodevelopmental delay.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Complications of neonatal respiratory distress syndrome are related mainly to the clinical course of RDS in neonates and the long term outcomes of the neonates. While surfactant therapy has decreased the morbidity associated with RDS, many patients continue to have complications during and after the acute course of RDS.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: The beneficial effect of antenatal corticosteroid use after 34 weeks of gestation is controversial due to the lack of information in long term developmental outcomes. [44] Maternal antenatal corticosteroids are recommended for possible preterm delivery in the next seven days between 23rd and 34th week gestational age. [45] Some institutions offer antenatal corticosteroids at 22 weeks if anticipating delivery within the next week. Despite multiple interventions targeting various etiologies, the goal of preventing preterm birth remains elusive.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal respiratory distress syndrome (RDS) occurs from a deficiency of surfactant, due to either inadequate surfactant production, or surfactant inactivation in the context of immature lungs. Prematurity affects both these factors, thereby directly contributing to RDS.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Since the definition of neonatal respiratory distress syndrome is imprecise, prompt diagnosis and treatment require an overall assessment of prenatal and delivery history to identify perinatal risk factors, clinical presentation, radiographic findings, and evidence of hypoxemia on blood gas analysis. As described above, the clinical presentation consists of non-specific respiratory symptoms, including tachypnea, nasal flaring, grunting, retractions, and cyanosis, with decreased air entry on auscultation.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Review the etiology of neonatal respiratory distress syndrome. Outline the evaluation of neonatal respiratory distress syndrome. Summarize the management of respiratory distress syndrome in neonates. Describe the importance of interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and outcomes related to neonatal respiratory distress syndrome. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The goals of optimal management of neonatal respiratory distress syndrome include decreasing incidence and severity using antenatal corticosteroids, followed by optimal management using respiratory support, surfactant therapy, and overall care of the premature infant. Antenatal corticosteroids (discussed later in the topic) Monitoring oxygenation and ventilation Assisted ventilation of the neonate Exogenous surfactant therapy Supportive care, including thermoregulation, nutritional support, fluid and electrolyte management, antibiotic therapy, etc. [22] [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: BPD is a chronic complication of RDS. The pathophysiology of BPD involves both arrested lung development as well as lung injury and inflammation. Besides a surfactant deficiency, the immature lung of the premature infant has decreased compliance, decreased fluid clearance, and immature vascular development, which predisposes the lung to injury and inflammation, further disrupting the normal development of alveoli and pulmonary vasculature. Also, oxidative stress from hyperoxia secondary to mechanical ventilation, and decreased anti-oxidant capabilities of the premature lung, both lead to further damage to the lung through the increased production on TGF-β1 and other pro-inflammatory cytokines. [41]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1723
retrieved_docs.content: The term خديج designates infants who have completed 29–30 weeks of gestation, and this developmental stage is directly linked to the occurrence of متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة (pulmonary immaturity syndrome). Consequently, babies classified as خديج are at risk for respiratory complications arising from lung immaturity, highlighting the importance of recognizing this gestational age category when assessing and managing neonatal respiratory health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 508
retrieved_docs.content: reinforce the connection between neonatal respiratory compromise, extreme prematurity, and perinatal infection. This integrated hierarchy supports comprehensive patient care by aligning gestational age, birth weight, fetal growth, and respiratory complications with their appropriate diagnostic categories across ICD‑10‑AM, WHO perinatal codes, and multilingual clinical terminologies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 1 -- Community 223
retrieved_docs.content: The community describes a hierarchical framework that links gestational age, growth parameters, perinatal infections, and neonatal complications. Completion of 29‑30 weeks of gestation is classified as a preterm (خديج) condition and is considered a liveborn outcome; “liveborn” functions as a broad category that contains newborn disorders (اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة), which are a subclass of the preterm condition. Preterm birth is frequently associated with respiratory distress syndrome (متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة), also termed pulmonary surfactant deficiency syndrome, highlighting the causal link between early gestation and immature lung development. Respiratory distress syndrome is simultaneously classified as a respiratory disorder (اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ) and a lung disorder (اِضطِراب الرِّئَة), and is a member of the neonatal‑and‑newborn disorder set (اضطرابات لدى حديث الولادة, SMQ). In ICD‑10‑AM it maps to the code for اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ under the parent “Duration of gestation,” and within

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 533
retrieved_docs.content: how extreme prematurity, short gestation, low birth weight, fetal growth restriction, respiratory distress, perinatal infection, birth trauma, and neonatal metabolic disorders are interlinked within a multilingual, multi‑coded framework that supports comprehensive patient care and data interoperability.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: The medical framework links gestational age, birth outcomes, and perinatal disorders through a hierarchical classification system. A gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is identified as a precursor to a liveborn outcome, indicating that infants born after this duration can survive outside the womb. Duration of gestation is positioned as a broader category that inversely encompasses liveborn, underscoring that the length of pregnancy defines the viability of a live birth. Conditions associated with short gestation and low birth weight — collectively described as Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation — serve as a primary classifier for liveborn infants, reflecting the clinical importance of prematurity and insufficient fetal growth in determining neonatal status. This same condition is also classified as ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن} (“birth at the highest degree of prematurity, unspecified weight”), reinforcing its role as a marker of

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 508
retrieved_docs.content: disorder). Clinically, it is included within **اضطرابات لدى حديث الولادة (SMQ)**, the neonatal disorders grouping, and is mapped from **حالة غير محددة نشأت في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة** (unspecified condition arising in the perinatal period). This perinatal condition is linked to **عدوى في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة، غير محدد بخلاف ذلك** (nonspecific perinatal infection) and has a broader relationship with **تقييد النمو الجنيني** (fetal growth restriction). **Respiratory distress syndrome** is cross‑referenced with **متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة** (pulmonary immaturity syndrome). Within **اضطرابات لدى حديث الولادة (SMQ)**, additional members such as **ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}** (extreme prematurity of unspecified weight) and **عدوى في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة، غير محدد بخلاف ذلك** reinforce the connection between neonatal respiratory compromise, extreme prematurity, and perinatal infection. This integrated hierarchy supports comprehensive patient care by aligning gestational

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2763
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation constitute a severe perinatal condition that is classified as an extremely premature birth of the highest degree with unspecified weight (ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}). This classification highlights that the infant is born far before term, exhibits markedly reduced body weight, and shows evidence of impaired intra‑uterine growth, all of which place the newborn at high risk for morbidity and mortality and necessitate intensive neonatal monitoring and intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is associated with a liveborn infant, indicating that pregnancies reaching this stage can result in a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 102
retrieved_docs.content: and cardiovascular complications are a subset of the overall perinatal condition spectrum. Collectively, these interrelated entities illustrate how gestational age, birthweight, and fetal growth trajectories define a network of classifications—from specific gestational milestones (29‑30 weeks) to broad perinatal disorder groups—providing a comprehensive framework for understanding, diagnosing, and managing health issues in liveborn infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1707
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal disorder (اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة) is a broad category of conditions affecting newborns. Respiratory distress syndrome is identified as a specific type of neonatal disorder, and the category also includes the child conditions fetal erythroblastosis (كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية) and premature rupture of membranes (رضح الولادة). Prevention and control measures are applicable to the overall neonatal disorder as well as to each of its constituent conditions—respiratory distress syndrome, fetal erythroblastosis, and premature rupture of membranes—underscoring the importance of proactive management in newborn care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2862
retrieved_docs.content: Early or threatened labor is classified as تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر, indicating a condition in which signs of labor appear prematurely and the situation is identified as threatened preterm labor, a designation that guides clinical monitoring and intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1449
retrieved_docs.content: Preterm low‑birth‑weight infants are clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature). Short stature itself is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), indicating that children who remain short may have an increased risk of dying in the newborn period. Additionally, تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر (threatened preterm labor) is clinically associated with قصر القامة, suggesting that pregnancies complicated by early labor threats often result in offspring who develop short stature. Collectively, these relationships highlight short stature as a common clinical link among preterm low birth weight, threatened preterm labor, and adverse neonatal outcomes such as neonatal death, underscoring its relevance for risk assessment and early intervention in perinatal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 533
retrieved_docs.content: The community’s knowledge base describes a hierarchical taxonomy of perinatal and neonatal disorders that integrates gestational timing, birth weight, fetal growth, and specific clinical conditions across multiple languages and coding systems. A gestational period of 29–30 weeks is classified as خديج (preterm) and is a subtype of liveborn infant; خديج is causally linked to متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة (pulmonary surfactant deficiency syndrome) and to respiratory distress syndrome (RDS), which is a subtype of اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة (newborn disorder). اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة is a member of the SMQ group اضطرابات لدى حديث الولادة and is associated with عدوى في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة، غير محدد بخلاف ذلك (unspecified perinatal infection). Transient neonatal diabetes (DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL 1 and 2), developmental delay, epilepsy, and other neonatal metabolic disorders are also mapped to اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة. Disorders relating to short gestation and/or low birthweight are

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1830
retrieved_docs.content: اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة (neonatal disorder) is a broad clinical category that includes specific conditions such as respiratory distress syndrome and كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية (excess fetal red erythroblasts). The inverse_isa relationship indicates that respiratory distress syndrome is a subtype of اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة, while the has child relationship shows that كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية is a manifestation or sub‑condition within the same neonatal disorder. For each of these entities—اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة, respiratory distress syndrome, and كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية—the qualifier “etiologie” is allowed, meaning that the cause or etiology can be specified for each condition. Recognizing and describing the etiological factors of the neonatal disorder and its constituent conditions is essential for accurate diagnosis and targeted management in newborn care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2444
retrieved_docs.content: Fetal distress and abnormal forces of labor are identified as a specific form of ضائقة جنينية تؤثر في علاج الأم, indicating that this type of fetal compromise not only reflects problems with the fetus but also has direct implications for the management and therapeutic decisions concerning the mother. Recognizing that fetal distress and abnormal mechanical forces during labor can affect maternal treatment underscores the importance of coordinated obstetric care that addresses both fetal well‑being and maternal health outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1721
retrieved_docs.content: Infant preterm low birth weight, early or threatened labor, threatened preterm labor (تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر), short stature (قصر القامة), and neonatal death (وفاة حديث الولادة) are interrelated clinical concepts. Infant preterm low birth weight is clinically associated with short stature, indicating that babies born prematurely with low weight are more likely to develop reduced adult height. Short stature itself is clinically associated with neonatal death, suggesting that children who remain short have an increased risk of dying in the newborn period. Early or threatened labor is used to classify threatened preterm labor, and threatened preterm labor is also clinically associated with short stature. Together, these relationships highlight that low birth weight and threatened preterm labor can contribute to short stature, which in turn is linked to higher neonatal mortality, underscoring the importance of monitoring growth and labor complications in perinatal care.
